17. Write @ -
Input
{
  "content": "# Kubelet Container Manager: Subsystem Architecture Guide\n\n## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies\n\n### Purpose\n\nThe kubelet container manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm`) is the subsystem responsible for translating pod resource specifications into kernel-enforced constraints on the node. Its core responsibilities are:\n\n- Structuring the cgroup hierarchy that every pod and container lives under\n- Enforcing node-level resource reservations (system, kube-reserved, node allocatable)\n- Coordinating CPU, memory, and device allocation across competing pods\n- Providing topology-aware placement on multi-NUMA nodes\n- Bridging third-party hardware (via device plugins and DRA) into the container runtime\n\n### Boundaries\n\n**Upstream consumers:**\n- `pkg/kubelet/kubelet.go` constructs the manager via `cm.NewContainerManager` and calls `ContainerManager.Start()`.\n- `pkg/kubelet/lifecycle` calls `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` to gate pod admission.\n- Pod workers call `InternalContainerLifecycle().PreCreateContainer()` / `PreStartContainer()` / `PostStopContainer()` at each container lifecycle event.\n\n**Downstream dependencies:**\n- `github.com/opencontainers/cgroups` \u2014 kernel cgroup interactions (v1 and v2)\n- `k8s.io/cri-api/pkg/apis` (`RuntimeService`) \u2014 container runtime calls for resource updates\n- `github.com/google/cadvisor` \u2014 machine topology and capacity discovery\n- `pkg/kubelet/pluginmanager` \u2014 device plugin and DRA plugin registration\n\n### What the Manager Does Not Do\n\n- It does not schedule or select which node a pod lands on (that is the scheduler's role).\n- It does not execute containers \u2014 that is the container runtime's role.\n- It does not handle network or storage resource classes; those are separate kubelet subsystems.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split\n\nThe subsystem is composed of a central coordinator (`containerManagerImpl`) and several specialized sub-managers, each owning a distinct resource class.\n\n### 2.1 `ContainerManager` Interface\n\nDefined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go:66`.\n\nThis is the single surface that the rest of kubelet uses. Key method groups:\n\n| Method group | Purpose |\n|---|---|\n| `Start(...)` | Initialize all sub-managers and cgroup hierarchy at kubelet startup |\n| `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` | Returns the topology manager as a `PodAdmitHandler` |\n| `InternalContainerLifecycle()` | Container lifecycle hooks for allocation and cleanup |\n| `GetResources(ctx, pod, container)` | Retrieve device/DRA runtime options for a container |\n| `UpdatePluginResources(...)` | Gate pod admission against device plugin capacity |\n| `NewPodContainerManager()` | Factory for per-pod cgroup management |\n| `GetCapacity / GetDevicePluginResourceCapacity` | Report node resource totals to kubelet for scheduling |\n| `Updates()` | Channel delivering device/DRA health-change events |\n\n### 2.2 `containerManagerImpl` (Linux)\n\nDefined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go:100`.\n\nThe concrete Linux implementation embeds:\n\n```go\ntype containerManagerImpl struct {\n    cgroupManager   CgroupManager           // raw cgroup operations\n    qosContainerManager QOSContainerManager // Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort hierarchy\n    deviceManager   devicemanager.Manager\n    cpuManager      cpumanager.Manager\n    memoryManager   memorymanager.Manager\n    topologyManager topologymanager.Manager\n    draManager      *dra.Manager            // Dynamic Resource Allocation (feature-gated)\n    resourceUpdates chan resourceupdates.Update\n    // cadvisor, mountUtil, nodeInfo, subsystems, capacity, cgroupRoot, recorder ...\n}\n```\n\n### 2.3 `CgroupManager`\n\nInterface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go:64`.\n\nAbstracts over two kernel cgroup APIs:\n- **cgroupfs** (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go`, `cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go`) \u2014 direct filesystem writes\n- **systemd** (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_manager_linux.go`) \u2014 D-Bus unit management\n\n`CgroupName` is an internal path representation (`[]string{\"kubepods\",\"burstable\",\"podUID\"}`). `CgroupName.ToSystemd()` produces the systemd slice path; `ParseCgroupfsToCgroupName()` parses the cgroupfs path. The `-` vs `_` escaping scheme in `cgroup_manager_linux.go:68` is an intentional invariant: internal names forbid `_` so the systemd round-trip is always reversible.\n\n### 2.4 `QOSContainerManager`\n\nDefined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go:48`.\n\nMaintains three top-level cgroups under the `kubepods` root:\n- `kubepods/guaranteed`\n- `kubepods/burstable`\n- `kubepods/besteffort`\n\nEvery minute (`periodicQOSCgroupUpdateInterval = 1*time.Minute`) it recomputes and applies resource limits for the burstable and best-effort tiers based on current active-pod state. This ensures that best-effort workloads are bounded to whatever headroom remains after guaranteed and burstable pods are accounted for.\n\n### 2.5 `PodContainerManager`\n\nInterface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go:109`.\n\nOne instance per pod. `EnsureExists()` creates a pod-level cgroup leaf under the appropriate QoS parent. Destroyed when the pod is removed. If `CgroupsPerQOS` is false, the noop implementation (`pod_container_manager_stub.go`) is returned instead.\n\n### 2.6 CPU Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/`)\n\nManages exclusive CPU pinning for Guaranteed QoS containers with integer CPU requests. Core types:\n\n- `Manager` interface (`cpu_manager.go:56`) \u2014 lifecycle + `Allocate` + topology hints\n- `Policy` interface (`policy.go`) \u2014 pluggable allocation strategies\n- `state.State` interface (`state/state.go`) \u2014 persistent + in-memory allocation bookkeeping\n\nTwo policies ship:\n- **none** (`policy_none.go`) \u2014 all containers share the default CPUSet; no pinning\n- **static** (`policy_static.go`) \u2014 exclusively pins CPUs for eligible containers; maintains shared, reserved, and exclusive CPU pools\n\n### 2.7 Memory Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/`)\n\nManages NUMA-pinned memory allocation for Guaranteed QoS workloads, including hugepages. Follows the same three-layer design as the CPU manager: `Manager` \u2192 `Policy` \u2192 `State`. The static policy allocates memory blocks from specific NUMA nodes.\n\n### 2.8 Topology Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/`)\n\nThe cross-cutting coordinator. Defined in `topology_manager.go:58`, it implements `lifecycle.PodAdmitHandler` and is returned directly by `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` (`container_manager_linux.go:760`). All other resource managers register as `HintProvider` instances with the topology manager.\n\n### 2.9 Device Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/`)\n\nManages device plugin resources (GPUs, FPGAs, NICs, etc.) via the v1beta1 plugin API. Exposes topology hints when plugins report NUMA affinity and serves as the allocation backend for `GetResources`.\n\n### 2.10 DRA Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/`)\n\nFeature-gated (`DynamicResourceAllocation`). Implements the structured parameter model for third-party resource claims, complementing the device plugin model with richer API semantics.\n\n### 2.11 `InternalContainerLifecycle`\n\nInterface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle.go:28`. Implemented by `internalContainerLifecycleImpl` which holds references to the CPU, memory, and topology managers. The container runtime bridge calls these three hooks at the container lifecycle boundaries:\n\n```go\nPreCreateContainer(pod, container, containerConfig)  // modify CRI config (e.g. cpuset annotations)\nPreStartContainer(pod, container, containerID)        // register container ID \u2192 allocation\nPostStopContainer(containerID)                        // release allocation\n```\n\n---\n\n## 3. End-to-End Control Flow\n\n### 3.1 Initialization (`ContainerManager.Start`)\n\n`container_manager_linux.go:615`\n\n```\nkubelet calls cm.Start(ctx, node, activePods, getNode, sourcesReady, podStatusProvider, runtimeService, ...)\n\u2502\n\u251c\u2500 buildContainerMapAndRunningSetFromRuntime()     // snapshot existing containers from CRI\n\u251c\u2500 cm.draManager.Start(...)                         // if DRA feature gate enabled\n\u251c\u2500 cm.cpuManager.Start(...)                         // restore checkpoint, start reconcile loop\n\u251c\u2500 cm.memoryManager.Start(...)                      // restore checkpoint\n\u251c\u2500 validateNodeAllocatable()\n\u251c\u2500 setupNode(ctx, activePods)\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 validateSystemRequirements()               // check cgroup subsystem mounts\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 setupKernelTunables()                      // vm.overcommit_memory, etc.\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 createNodeAllocatableCgroups()             // mkdir kubepods/\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 qosContainerManager.Start()               // create Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort cgroups\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups()            // set kubepods/ limits; retries until eviction frees memory\n\u2502   \u2514\u2500 configure systemContainers (runtime, kubelet, system cgroups)\n\u2502\n\u251c\u2500 start periodic system container ensure-state goroutine (every 1 min)\n\u251c\u2500 start periodic task goroutine (every 5 min)\n\u2514\u2500 cm.deviceManager.Start(...)                    // connect to device plugin sockets, reconcile\n```\n\n**Manager construction order matters** (`container_manager_linux.go:296`):\n1. Topology manager created first (it holds no references to other managers)\n2. Device manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`\n3. CPU manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`\n4. Memory manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`\n\nThis ordering ensures the topology manager's `Store` interface is available when sub-managers need to query the merged affinity hint.\n\n### 3.2 Pod Admission Flow\n\n```\nkubelet admission loop\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 lifecycle.PodAdmitHandler.Admit(attrs)\n    \u2502  (handler is cm.topologyManager, returned by GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler())\n    \u2502\n    \u2514\u2500 topologymanager.Admit(attrs)          // topology_manager.go\n        \u2502\n        \u251c\u2500 For each container (container scope) OR for the pod (pod scope):\n        \u2502   \u251c\u2500 Collect hints: cpuManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)\n        \u2502   \u251c\u2500 Collect hints: memoryManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)\n        \u2502   \u251c\u2500 Collect hints: deviceManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)\n        \u2502   \u2514\u2500 Merge hints per policy \u2192 best TopologyHint stored in scope.podTopologyHints\n        \u2502\n        \u2514\u2500 For each container:\n            \u251c\u2500 Call cpuManager.Allocate(pod, container)\n            \u2502   \u2514\u2500 policy.Allocate(s, pod, container)\n            \u2502       \u2514\u2500 (static policy) query topologyManager.GetAffinity() for NUMA mask\n            \u2502          \u2192 allocateCPUs() using cpuAccumulator (cpu_assignment.go)\n            \u2502          \u2192 persist to state (checkpoint)\n            \u251c\u2500 Call memoryManager.Allocate(pod, container)\n            \u2502   \u2514\u2500 policy.Allocate(s, pod, container)\n            \u2502       \u2514\u2500 query topologyManager.GetAffinity() for NUMA mask\n            \u2502          \u2192 allocate memory blocks from matching NUMA nodes\n            \u2502          \u2192 persist to state (checkpoint)\n            \u2514\u2500 Call deviceManager.Allocate(pod, container)\n                \u2514\u2500 contact device plugin via gRPC Allocate() RPC\n                   \u2192 store DeviceRunContainerOptions in checkpoint\n```\n\n**Key invariant:** The topology manager calls `GetTopologyHints` to *compute* the merged NUMA affinity and stores it. It then calls `Allocate` on each hint provider, which *reads back* the stored affinity via `Store.GetAffinity(podUID, containerName)`. The two-phase design prevents circular dependencies between managers.\n\nIf no feasible intersection of NUMA hints exists under the chosen policy, the topology manager returns a `TopologyAffinityError`, and the pod is rejected at admission with a descriptive event.\n\n### 3.3 Container Start Flow\n\n```\nPod worker \u2192 container runtime manager\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 InternalContainerLifecycle().PreCreateContainer(pod, container, containerConfig)\n    \u2514\u2500 (Linux: internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go)\n       cpuset annotation or CRI cpu-set fields populated from cpu manager state\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 CRI CreateContainer()\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 InternalContainerLifecycle().PreStartContainer(pod, container, containerID)\n    \u251c\u2500 cpuManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID)   // register ID\u2192alloc mapping\n    \u251c\u2500 memoryManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID) // same\n    \u2514\u2500 topologyManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID) // track for cleanup\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 CRI StartContainer()\n```\n\n### 3.4 CPU Reconciliation Loop\n\nStarted inside `cpuManager.Start()`. Runs every `CPUManagerReconcilePeriod` (default 10 s, configurable via `--cpu-manager-reconcile-period`):\n\n```\nreconcileState()\n\u2502\n\u251c\u2500 for each active pod/container:\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 look up assigned CPUSet in state\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 look up last-applied CPUSet in lastUpdateState\n\u2502   \u2514\u2500 if different: runtimeService.UpdateContainerResources(containerID, cpuSet)\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 for stale containers (in state but not in active pods, once sourcesReady):\n    \u2514\u2500 policy.RemoveContainer(state, podUID, containerName)\n```\n\nThis loop is the safety net: even if the `PreCreateContainer` path missed a container (e.g., kubelet restart), the reconcile loop re-establishes the correct CPU affinity.\n\n### 3.5 Container Stop Flow\n\n```\nPod worker terminates container\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 InternalContainerLifecycle().PostStopContainer(containerID)\n    \u2514\u2500 topologyManager.RemoveContainer(containerID)\n        \u2514\u2500 scope.RemoveContainer(containerID)  // remove from podTopologyHints map\n```\n\nCPU and memory allocations are cleaned up lazily in the reconcile loop when the container is no longer in the active pods set.\n\n### 3.6 Resource Update Channel\n\n`container_manager_linux.go:360\u2013389` sets up a fan-in goroutine that merges update channels from device manager and DRA manager into a single `chan resourceupdates.Update`. The kubelet main sync loop consumes `cm.Updates()` to trigger pod status re-evaluation when device health changes.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs\n\n### 4.1 Topology Manager NUMA Node Limit\n\n`topology_manager.go:42`:\n```go\ndefaultMaxAllowableNUMANodes = 8\n```\nThe hint-merging algorithm enumerates all permutations of hints across providers. With *N* NUMA nodes, each provider can generate up to 2^N \u2212 1 possible affinity masks. With 8 NUMA nodes and 3 providers, the state space is manageable; beyond 8 nodes, initialization fails with an error rather than produce unacceptably slow admission checks. This is a hard architectural limit.\n\n### 4.2 CPU Manager Static Policy Constraints\n\n- **Integer CPUs only**: Fractional CPU requests never receive exclusive pinning. A container requesting `0.5` CPU shares the pool.\n- **Reserved CPUs must be non-zero**: The shared pool is defined as `allCPUs \u2212 reserved \u2212 exclusive`. If no CPUs are reserved and all guaranteed pods claim all CPUs, best-effort pods may starve. The policy validation prevents `ReservedSystemCPUs` from being empty on nodes with Guaranteed pods.\n- **SMT alignment**: The `FullPhysicalCPUsOnly` option prevents allocating threads from the same physical core to different containers, eliminating noisy-neighbor cache interference at the cost of lower packing density.\n\n### 4.3 Checkpoint Persistence vs. In-Memory State\n\nCPU, memory, and device managers all use a checkpoint file under `KubeletRootDir` (e.g., `/var/lib/kubelet/cpu_manager_state`). On kubelet restart:\n- Allocations from prior containers are restored.\n- If the checkpoint format changes across versions, the manager performs a policy-name comparison and resets state if inconsistent.\n- Loss of the checkpoint directory (e.g., ephemeral-root node) means all allocations are forgotten; the reconcile loop re-establishes CPU affinity for running containers but cannot recover device allocations for deleted pods.\n\n### 4.4 Node Allocatable Enforcement Race\n\n`node_container_manager_linux.go:95` shows that if the node has running pods whose memory usage exceeds the new `kubepods` cgroup limit (e.g., after lowering `--kube-reserved`), the `cgroupManager.Update()` call will fail. The code retries in a goroutine indefinitely until eviction frees memory. This retry loop does not have a deadline, so the node may emit repeated warning events for an extended period during eviction. Memory limit enforcement is therefore best-effort with eventual consistency.\n\n### 4.5 cgroup v1 Deprecation\n\n`container_manager.go:52`:\n```go\nCgroupV1DeprecatedWarning = \"cgroup v1 detected. cgroup v1 support is deprecated and will be removed in a future release.\"\n```\nThe system validates required subsystem mounts at startup (`validateSystemRequirements`). Under cgroup v2 unified hierarchy, cpu quota hard-capping is unconditionally available. Under cgroup v1, cpu quota requires both `cpu.cfs_period_us` and `cpu.cfs_quota_us` to be present; their absence degrades to soft CPU limits only (`cm.status.SoftRequirements`).\n\n### 4.6 Device Plugin Health Propagation\n\nWhen a device transitions from healthy to unhealthy, the device manager sends a `resourceupdates.Update` on its channel. The kubelet sync loop re-evaluates affected pods. However, previously allocated unhealthy devices are not automatically revoked from running containers \u2014 the device plugin is responsible for handling its own device failure semantics. This is a deliberate interface boundary: the device manager tracks status but does not forcibly kill containers when hardware fails.\n\n### 4.7 DRA vs. Device Plugin Coexistence\n\nBoth DRA (`draManager`) and classic device plugins (`deviceManager`) can be active simultaneously (controlled by the `DynamicResourceAllocation` feature gate). They use different plugin registration handlers (`DRAPlugin` vs. `DevicePlugin` in `GetPluginRegistrationHandlers`). A resource requested via DRA claims is handled entirely by `draManager.GetResources()` and never passes through `deviceManager`. The two paths do not interfere but cannot share a device instance.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Extension Points, Customization Hooks, and Associated Risks\n\n### 5.1 CPU Manager Policies via Options\n\nThe static CPU policy accepts a `CPUManagerPolicyOptions` map passed at kubelet startup. Options such as `full-pcpus-only`, `distribute-cpus-across-numa`, and `prefer-align-cpus-by-uncore-cache` alter the `cpuAccumulator` allocation strategy in `cpu_assignment.go` without requiring a different policy name. Adding a new option only requires implementing a new code path inside `policy_static.go:Allocate` and declaring the option in the feature-gated options registry.\n\n**Risk**: Options interact. `distribute-cpus-across-numa` and `full-pcpus-only` together may produce no valid allocation on nodes with odd CPU counts, causing guaranteed pods to be perpetually rejected. There is no static validation of option combinations; failures surface only at runtime as admission errors.\n\n### 5.2 Topology Manager Policies\n\nFour policy implementations exist in `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/`: `none`, `best-effort`, `restricted`, `single-numa-node`. They share the `HintMerger` in `policy.go` but differ in how they handle non-preferred hints. New policies can be added by implementing the `Policy` interface (`Name() string; Merge(logger, providersHints) (TopologyHint, bool)`) and registering in `NewManager`.\n\n**Risk**: A custom policy that always returns `preferred=true` for any affinity mask will never reject a pod, silently undermining NUMA locality guarantees without surfacing errors.\n\n### 5.3 Topology Manager Scopes\n\nTwo scopes exist (`scope_container.go`, `scope_pod.go`). Pod scope aggregates hints across all containers before merging; container scope merges per-container independently. Switching from container to pod scope on a running node with allocated Guaranteed pods requires draining the node, because pod scope may accept pods that container scope would reject (or vice versa).\n\n### 5.4 Device Plugins\n\nThird-party device plugins implement the v1beta1 gRPC API in `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/plugin/v1beta1/`. Plugins must implement `ListAndWatch`, `Allocate`, and optionally `GetTopologyHints`. The device manager calls `GetTopologyHints` during the topology manager's admission phase; a plugin that returns inaccurate NUMA topology hints will cause the topology manager to make suboptimal placements without any error signal.\n\nPlugins that return non-reproducible allocations across `Allocate` calls break the checkpoint restore invariant: after kubelet restart, the checkpoint may contain a device ID that the plugin no longer knows about.\n\n### 5.5 `HintProvider` Registration\n\n`cm.topologyManager.AddHintProvider(logger, provider)` in `container_manager_linux.go:312,341,356` is called during construction. This is not a runtime extension point; there is no way to register a new hint provider after `NewContainerManager` returns. In-tree managers are the only registered providers today.\n\n### 5.6 `InternalContainerLifecycle` Hooks\n\n`PreCreateContainer` in `internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go` is the place where CRI container config is modified (e.g., to inject CPU affinity annotations). Adding a new resource type that needs CRI-level config injection requires extending this hook. The interface is internal (`package cm`) and not a stable extension point for external components.\n\n---\n\n## 6. Operational Implications\n\n### 6.1 Performance\n\n**Admission latency**: Every pod admission call traverses the topology manager, which in turn calls `GetTopologyHints` on all registered providers synchronously. For a cluster with 3 resource managers and up to 8 NUMA nodes, the worst-case hint permutation count is O((2^8)^3) \u2248 16M. In practice, constraints reduce this by orders of magnitude, but nodes with many NUMA nodes and many resource types will exhibit measurable admission latency. Monitor `kubelet_topology_manager_admission_duration_ms` (emitted via `metrics`) to detect regressions.\n\n**CPU reconcile loop overhead**: The reconcile loop calls `runtimeService.UpdateContainerResources()` for every container whose CPU assignment differs from the last recorded state. On a node with hundreds of containers, the loop may generate significant CRI traffic every `CPUManagerReconcilePeriod`. Widen the period (`--cpu-manager-reconcile-period`) to reduce CRI overhead if CPU affinity changes are infrequent in your workload.\n\n**QoS cgroup update interval**: `qosContainerManager` recomputes burstable and best-effort cgroup limits every minute. On a busy node with rapidly changing pod counts, this update may arrive 60 s after a pod terminates, leaving residual memory headroom unclaimed. This is acceptable for typical workloads but can cause transient OOM pressure immediately after high-density pod churn.\n\n### 6.2 Reliability\n\n**Kubelet restart recovery**: CPU and memory managers restore state from checkpoint before `sourcesReady` is declared. Until sources are ready, stale state removal is suppressed (`reconcileState` skips removal). This window ensures that containers started by a prior kubelet invocation are not deprived of their CPU affinity during the brief period when pod sources are still being synchronized.\n\n**Device plugin disconnection**: The device manager uses gRPC streaming (`ListAndWatch`) to receive device health updates. If the plugin crashes or disconnects, its devices are immediately marked unhealthy in the manager's internal map. Capacity reported via `GetDevicePluginResourceCapacity` drops to zero for that resource, preventing new pods from being scheduled to the node. Running pods retain their device files \u2014 the kernel does not revoke them \u2014 but the plugin is no longer available to handle `Allocate` for new containers.\n\n**cgroup write failures**: `enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups` retries memory limit writes indefinitely when running pods' memory exceeds the target limit. This is the intended behavior: forcibly setting a lower limit would immediately OOM-kill running workloads. However, if eviction is misconfigured or stalled, the node will emit repeated `FailedNodeAllocatableEnforcement` events without ever converging. Operators should monitor for this event as a signal that eviction thresholds need tuning.\n\n### 6.3 Maintainability\n\n**Platform divergence**: `containerManagerImpl` has separate concrete implementations under build tags `linux`, `windows`, and the stub (`container_manager_stub.go`). The Windows implementation (`container_manager_windows.go`) contains a minimal struct with `deviceManager`, `topologyManager`, `cpuManager`, and `memoryManager` fields, but cgroup enforcement and QoS hierarchy are absent. Any change to `ContainerManager` interface in `container_manager.go` must be reflected in all three platform implementations. The mock in `pkg/kubelet/cm/testing/mocks.go` is auto-generated from the interface and must be regenerated after interface changes (`//go:generate mockery` in `container_manager.go:17`).\n\n**State schema evolution**: Checkpoint files embed a policy name. If the policy name changes (e.g., a rename of `\"static\"` to `\"pinned\"`), kubelet will consider the checkpoint invalid and reset all allocations at startup. This means all Guaranteed pods must be restarted to regain exclusive CPU/memory pinning after such a policy rename.\n\n---\n\n## 7. File and Directory Map\n\n| Path | Role |\n|---|---|\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go` | `ContainerManager` interface definition; `NodeConfig`, `NodeAllocatableConfig`, `Status` types |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go` | Linux `containerManagerImpl`: construction, `Start()`, wiring of sub-managers |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_windows.go` | Windows stub: minimal `containerManagerImpl`, no cgroup hierarchy |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_stub.go` | Unsupported-platform stub |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go` | `CgroupManager`, `PodContainerManager`, `CgroupName`, `ResourceConfig`, `CgroupConfig` interfaces and types |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle.go` | `InternalContainerLifecycle` interface; `internalContainerLifecycleImpl` implementation |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go` | Linux-specific `PreCreateContainer` (CRI config injection) |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_manager_linux.go` | `CgroupName.ToSystemd()`, name escaping, `NewCgroupManager` dispatch |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go` | cgroupfs v1 CRUD operations |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go` | cgroupfs v2 unified hierarchy CRUD operations |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go` | `QOSContainerManager`: Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort cgroup management |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/node_container_manager_linux.go` | Node allocatable cgroup creation and enforcement with retry logic |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/pod_container_manager_linux.go` | `PodContainerManager`: per-pod cgroup lifecycle |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/cpu_manager.go` | CPU `Manager` interface; `manager` struct; reconcile loop |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/policy_static.go` | Static CPU policy: `Allocate`, `allocateCPUs`, NUMA-aware assignment |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/cpu_assignment.go` | `cpuAccumulator`: topology-aware CPU selection algorithm |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/state/state_checkpoint.go` | Checkpoint-backed state persistence |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/memory_manager.go` | Memory `Manager` interface and implementation |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/policy_static.go` | Static memory policy: NUMA-pinned allocation |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/topology_manager.go` | `Manager` and `HintProvider` interfaces; `TopologyAffinityError`; NUMA node limit |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/policy.go` | `HintMerger`; hint permutation and scoring logic |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/scope_container.go` | Container-scope admission logic |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/scope_pod.go` | Pod-scope admission logic |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/bitmask/bitmask.go` | NUMA affinity bitmask operations |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/manager.go` | Device plugin `Manager` implementation; health tracking; allocation |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/pod_devices.go` | Per-pod device allocation tracking and checkpoint |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/topology_hints.go` | Device topology hint generation |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/plugin/v1beta1/` | Device plugin gRPC server/client and registration |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/` | Dynamic Resource Allocation manager (feature-gated) |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/resourceupdates/` | `Update` type for device/DRA health change events |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/admission/errors.go` | Admission error helpers |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/testing/mocks.go` | Auto-generated mock for `ContainerManager` (used in unit tests) |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/containermap/` | `ContainerMap` type: maps container IDs to pod UID + name during restart recovery |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/util/` | Helpers: cgroup path utilities |\n",
  "file_path": "/workspace/documentation.md"
}
Output
{
  "content": "# Kubelet Container Manager: Subsystem Architecture Guide\n\n## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies\n\n### Purpose\n\nThe kubelet container manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm`) is the subsystem responsible for translating pod resource specifications into kernel-enforced constraints on the node. Its core responsibilities are:\n\n- Structuring the cgroup hierarchy that every pod and container lives under\n- Enforcing node-level resource reservations (system, kube-reserved, node allocatable)\n- Coordinating CPU, memory, and device allocation across competing pods\n- Providing topology-aware placement on multi-NUMA nodes\n- Bridging third-party hardware (via device plugins and DRA) into the container runtime\n\n### Boundaries\n\n**Upstream consumers:**\n- `pkg/kubelet/kubelet.go` constructs the manager via `cm.NewContainerManager` and calls `ContainerManager.Start()`.\n- `pkg/kubelet/lifecycle` calls `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` to gate pod admission.\n- Pod workers call `InternalContainerLifecycle().PreCreateContainer()` / `PreStartContainer()` / `PostStopContainer()` at each container lifecycle event.\n\n**Downstream dependencies:**\n- `github.com/opencontainers/cgroups` \u2014 kernel cgroup interactions (v1 and v2)\n- `k8s.io/cri-api/pkg/apis` (`RuntimeService`) \u2014 container runtime calls for resource updates\n- `github.com/google/cadvisor` \u2014 machine topology and capacity discovery\n- `pkg/kubelet/pluginmanager` \u2014 device plugin and DRA plugin registration\n\n### What the Manager Does Not Do\n\n- It does not schedule or select which node a pod lands on (that is the scheduler's role).\n- It does not execute containers \u2014 that is the container runtime's role.\n- It does not handle network or storage resource classes; those are separate kubelet subsystems.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split\n\nThe subsystem is composed of a central coordinator (`containerManagerImpl`) and several specialized sub-managers, each owning a distinct resource class.\n\n### 2.1 `ContainerManager` Interface\n\nDefined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go:66`.\n\nThis is the single surface that the rest of kubelet uses. Key method groups:\n\n| Method group | Purpose |\n|---|---|\n| `Start(...)` | Initialize all sub-managers and cgroup hierarchy at kubelet startup |\n| `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` | Returns the topology manager as a `PodAdmitHandler` |\n| `InternalContainerLifecycle()` | Container lifecycle hooks for allocation and cleanup |\n| `GetResources(ctx, pod, container)` | Retrieve device/DRA runtime options for a container |\n| `UpdatePluginResources(...)` | Gate pod admission against device plugin capacity |\n| `NewPodContainerManager()` | Factory for per-pod cgroup management |\n| `GetCapacity / GetDevicePluginResourceCapacity` | Report node resource totals to kubelet for scheduling |\n| `Updates()` | Channel delivering device/DRA health-change events |\n\n### 2.2 `containerManagerImpl` (Linux)\n\nDefined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go:100`.\n\nThe concrete Linux implementation embeds:\n\n```go\ntype containerManagerImpl struct {\n    cgroupManager   CgroupManager           // raw cgroup operations\n    qosContainerManager QOSContainerManager // Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort hierarchy\n    deviceManager   devicemanager.Manager\n    cpuManager      cpumanager.Manager\n    memoryManager   memorymanager.Manager\n    topologyManager topologymanager.Manager\n    draManager      *dra.Manager            // Dynamic Resource Allocation (feature-gated)\n    resourceUpdates chan resourceupdates.Update\n    // cadvisor, mountUtil, nodeInfo, subsystems, capacity, cgroupRoot, recorder ...\n}\n```\n\n### 2.3 `CgroupManager`\n\nInterface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go:64`.\n\nAbstracts over two kernel cgroup APIs:\n- **cgroupfs** (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go`, `cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go`) \u2014 direct filesystem writes\n- **systemd** (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_manager_linux.go`) \u2014 D-Bus unit management\n\n`CgroupName` is an internal path representation (`[]string{\"kubepods\",\"burstable\",\"podUID\"}`). `CgroupName.ToSystemd()` produces the systemd slice path; `ParseCgroupfsToCgroupName()` parses the cgroupfs path. The `-` vs `_` escaping scheme in `cgroup_manager_linux.go:68` is an intentional invariant: internal names forbid `_` so the systemd round-trip is always reversible.\n\n### 2.4 `QOSContainerManager`\n\nDefined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go:48`.\n\nMaintains three top-level cgroups under the `kubepods` root:\n- `kubepods/guaranteed`\n- `kubepods/burstable`\n- `kubepods/besteffort`\n\nEvery minute (`periodicQOSCgroupUpdateInterval = 1*time.Minute`) it recomputes and applies resource limits for the burstable and best-effort tiers based on current active-pod state. This ensures that best-effort workloads are bounded to whatever headroom remains after guaranteed and burstable pods are accounted for.\n\n### 2.5 `PodContainerManager`\n\nInterface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go:109`.\n\nOne instance per pod. `EnsureExists()` creates a pod-level cgroup leaf under the appropriate QoS parent. Destroyed when the pod is removed. If `CgroupsPerQOS` is false, the noop implementation (`pod_container_manager_stub.go`) is returned instead.\n\n### 2.6 CPU Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/`)\n\nManages exclusive CPU pinning for Guaranteed QoS containers with integer CPU requests. Core types:\n\n- `Manager` interface (`cpu_manager.go:56`) \u2014 lifecycle + `Allocate` + topology hints\n- `Policy` interface (`policy.go`) \u2014 pluggable allocation strategies\n- `state.State` interface (`state/state.go`) \u2014 persistent + in-memory allocation bookkeeping\n\nTwo policies ship:\n- **none** (`policy_none.go`) \u2014 all containers share the default CPUSet; no pinning\n- **static** (`policy_static.go`) \u2014 exclusively pins CPUs for eligible containers; maintains shared, reserved, and exclusive CPU pools\n\n### 2.7 Memory Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/`)\n\nManages NUMA-pinned memory allocation for Guaranteed QoS workloads, including hugepages. Follows the same three-layer design as the CPU manager: `Manager` \u2192 `Policy` \u2192 `State`. The static policy allocates memory blocks from specific NUMA nodes.\n\n### 2.8 Topology Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/`)\n\nThe cross-cutting coordinator. Defined in `topology_manager.go:58`, it implements `lifecycle.PodAdmitHandler` and is returned directly by `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` (`container_manager_linux.go:760`). All other resource managers register as `HintProvider` instances with the topology manager.\n\n### 2.9 Device Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/`)\n\nManages device plugin resources (GPUs, FPGAs, NICs, etc.) via the v1beta1 plugin API. Exposes topology hints when plugins report NUMA affinity and serves as the allocation backend for `GetResources`.\n\n### 2.10 DRA Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/`)\n\nFeature-gated (`DynamicResourceAllocation`). Implements the structured parameter model for third-party resource claims, complementing the device plugin model with richer API semantics.\n\n### 2.11 `InternalContainerLifecycle`\n\nInterface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle.go:28`. Implemented by `internalContainerLifecycleImpl` which holds references to the CPU, memory, and topology managers. The container runtime bridge calls these three hooks at the container lifecycle boundaries:\n\n```go\nPreCreateContainer(pod, container, containerConfig)  // modify CRI config (e.g. cpuset annotations)\nPreStartContainer(pod, container, containerID)        // register container ID \u2192 allocation\nPostStopContainer(containerID)                        // release allocation\n```\n\n---\n\n## 3. End-to-End Control Flow\n\n### 3.1 Initialization (`ContainerManager.Start`)\n\n`container_manager_linux.go:615`\n\n```\nkubelet calls cm.Start(ctx, node, activePods, getNode, sourcesReady, podStatusProvider, runtimeService, ...)\n\u2502\n\u251c\u2500 buildContainerMapAndRunningSetFromRuntime()     // snapshot existing containers from CRI\n\u251c\u2500 cm.draManager.Start(...)                         // if DRA feature gate enabled\n\u251c\u2500 cm.cpuManager.Start(...)                         // restore checkpoint, start reconcile loop\n\u251c\u2500 cm.memoryManager.Start(...)                      // restore checkpoint\n\u251c\u2500 validateNodeAllocatable()\n\u251c\u2500 setupNode(ctx, activePods)\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 validateSystemRequirements()               // check cgroup subsystem mounts\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 setupKernelTunables()                      // vm.overcommit_memory, etc.\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 createNodeAllocatableCgroups()             // mkdir kubepods/\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 qosContainerManager.Start()               // create Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort cgroups\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups()            // set kubepods/ limits; retries until eviction frees memory\n\u2502   \u2514\u2500 configure systemContainers (runtime, kubelet, system cgroups)\n\u2502\n\u251c\u2500 start periodic system container ensure-state goroutine (every 1 min)\n\u251c\u2500 start periodic task goroutine (every 5 min)\n\u2514\u2500 cm.deviceManager.Start(...)                    // connect to device plugin sockets, reconcile\n```\n\n**Manager construction order matters** (`container_manager_linux.go:296`):\n1. Topology manager created first (it holds no references to other managers)\n2. Device manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`\n3. CPU manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`\n4. Memory manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`\n\nThis ordering ensures the topology manager's `Store` interface is available when sub-managers need to query the merged affinity hint.\n\n### 3.2 Pod Admission Flow\n\n```\nkubelet admission loop\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 lifecycle.PodAdmitHandler.Admit(attrs)\n    \u2502  (handler is cm.topologyManager, returned by GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler())\n    \u2502\n    \u2514\u2500 topologymanager.Admit(attrs)          // topology_manager.go\n        \u2502\n        \u251c\u2500 For each container (container scope) OR for the pod (pod scope):\n        \u2502   \u251c\u2500 Collect hints: cpuManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)\n        \u2502   \u251c\u2500 Collect hints: memoryManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)\n        \u2502   \u251c\u2500 Collect hints: deviceManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)\n        \u2502   \u2514\u2500 Merge hints per policy \u2192 best TopologyHint stored in scope.podTopologyHints\n        \u2502\n        \u2514\u2500 For each container:\n            \u251c\u2500 Call cpuManager.Allocate(pod, container)\n            \u2502   \u2514\u2500 policy.Allocate(s, pod, container)\n            \u2502       \u2514\u2500 (static policy) query topologyManager.GetAffinity() for NUMA mask\n            \u2502          \u2192 allocateCPUs() using cpuAccumulator (cpu_assignment.go)\n            \u2502          \u2192 persist to state (checkpoint)\n            \u251c\u2500 Call memoryManager.Allocate(pod, container)\n            \u2502   \u2514\u2500 policy.Allocate(s, pod, container)\n            \u2502       \u2514\u2500 query topologyManager.GetAffinity() for NUMA mask\n            \u2502          \u2192 allocate memory blocks from matching NUMA nodes\n            \u2502          \u2192 persist to state (checkpoint)\n            \u2514\u2500 Call deviceManager.Allocate(pod, container)\n                \u2514\u2500 contact device plugin via gRPC Allocate() RPC\n                   \u2192 store DeviceRunContainerOptions in checkpoint\n```\n\n**Key invariant:** The topology manager calls `GetTopologyHints` to *compute* the merged NUMA affinity and stores it. It then calls `Allocate` on each hint provider, which *reads back* the stored affinity via `Store.GetAffinity(podUID, containerName)`. The two-phase design prevents circular dependencies between managers.\n\nIf no feasible intersection of NUMA hints exists under the chosen policy, the topology manager returns a `TopologyAffinityError`, and the pod is rejected at admission with a descriptive event.\n\n### 3.3 Container Start Flow\n\n```\nPod worker \u2192 container runtime manager\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 InternalContainerLifecycle().PreCreateContainer(pod, container, containerConfig)\n    \u2514\u2500 (Linux: internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go)\n       cpuset annotation or CRI cpu-set fields populated from cpu manager state\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 CRI CreateContainer()\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 InternalContainerLifecycle().PreStartContainer(pod, container, containerID)\n    \u251c\u2500 cpuManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID)   // register ID\u2192alloc mapping\n    \u251c\u2500 memoryManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID) // same\n    \u2514\u2500 topologyManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID) // track for cleanup\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 CRI StartContainer()\n```\n\n### 3.4 CPU Reconciliation Loop\n\nStarted inside `cpuManager.Start()`. Runs every `CPUManagerReconcilePeriod` (default 10 s, configurable via `--cpu-manager-reconcile-period`):\n\n```\nreconcileState()\n\u2502\n\u251c\u2500 for each active pod/container:\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 look up assigned CPUSet in state\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500 look up last-applied CPUSet in lastUpdateState\n\u2502   \u2514\u2500 if different: runtimeService.UpdateContainerResources(containerID, cpuSet)\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 for stale containers (in state but not in active pods, once sourcesReady):\n    \u2514\u2500 policy.RemoveContainer(state, podUID, containerName)\n```\n\nThis loop is the safety net: even if the `PreCreateContainer` path missed a container (e.g., kubelet restart), the reconcile loop re-establishes the correct CPU affinity.\n\n### 3.5 Container Stop Flow\n\n```\nPod worker terminates container\n\u2502\n\u2514\u2500 InternalContainerLifecycle().PostStopContainer(containerID)\n    \u2514\u2500 topologyManager.RemoveContainer(containerID)\n        \u2514\u2500 scope.RemoveContainer(containerID)  // remove from podTopologyHints map\n```\n\nCPU and memory allocations are cleaned up lazily in the reconcile loop when the container is no longer in the active pods set.\n\n### 3.6 Resource Update Channel\n\n`container_manager_linux.go:360\u2013389` sets up a fan-in goroutine that merges update channels from device manager and DRA manager into a single `chan resourceupdates.Update`. The kubelet main sync loop consumes `cm.Updates()` to trigger pod status re-evaluation when device health changes.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs\n\n### 4.1 Topology Manager NUMA Node Limit\n\n`topology_manager.go:42`:\n```go\ndefaultMaxAllowableNUMANodes = 8\n```\nThe hint-merging algorithm enumerates all permutations of hints across providers. With *N* NUMA nodes, each provider can generate up to 2^N \u2212 1 possible affinity masks. With 8 NUMA nodes and 3 providers, the state space is manageable; beyond 8 nodes, initialization fails with an error rather than produce unacceptably slow admission checks. This is a hard architectural limit.\n\n### 4.2 CPU Manager Static Policy Constraints\n\n- **Integer CPUs only**: Fractional CPU requests never receive exclusive pinning. A container requesting `0.5` CPU shares the pool.\n- **Reserved CPUs must be non-zero**: The shared pool is defined as `allCPUs \u2212 reserved \u2212 exclusive`. If no CPUs are reserved and all guaranteed pods claim all CPUs, best-effort pods may starve. The policy validation prevents `ReservedSystemCPUs` from being empty on nodes with Guaranteed pods.\n- **SMT alignment**: The `FullPhysicalCPUsOnly` option prevents allocating threads from the same physical core to different containers, eliminating noisy-neighbor cache interference at the cost of lower packing density.\n\n### 4.3 Checkpoint Persistence vs. In-Memory State\n\nCPU, memory, and device managers all use a checkpoint file under `KubeletRootDir` (e.g., `/var/lib/kubelet/cpu_manager_state`). On kubelet restart:\n- Allocations from prior containers are restored.\n- If the checkpoint format changes across versions, the manager performs a policy-name comparison and resets state if inconsistent.\n- Loss of the checkpoint directory (e.g., ephemeral-root node) means all allocations are forgotten; the reconcile loop re-establishes CPU affinity for running containers but cannot recover device allocations for deleted pods.\n\n### 4.4 Node Allocatable Enforcement Race\n\n`node_container_manager_linux.go:95` shows that if the node has running pods whose memory usage exceeds the new `kubepods` cgroup limit (e.g., after lowering `--kube-reserved`), the `cgroupManager.Update()` call will fail. The code retries in a goroutine indefinitely until eviction frees memory. This retry loop does not have a deadline, so the node may emit repeated warning events for an extended period during eviction. Memory limit enforcement is therefore best-effort with eventual consistency.\n\n### 4.5 cgroup v1 Deprecation\n\n`container_manager.go:52`:\n```go\nCgroupV1DeprecatedWarning = \"cgroup v1 detected. cgroup v1 support is deprecated and will be removed in a future release.\"\n```\nThe system validates required subsystem mounts at startup (`validateSystemRequirements`). Under cgroup v2 unified hierarchy, cpu quota hard-capping is unconditionally available. Under cgroup v1, cpu quota requires both `cpu.cfs_period_us` and `cpu.cfs_quota_us` to be present; their absence degrades to soft CPU limits only (`cm.status.SoftRequirements`).\n\n### 4.6 Device Plugin Health Propagation\n\nWhen a device transitions from healthy to unhealthy, the device manager sends a `resourceupdates.Update` on its channel. The kubelet sync loop re-evaluates affected pods. However, previously allocated unhealthy devices are not automatically revoked from running containers \u2014 the device plugin is responsible for handling its own device failure semantics. This is a deliberate interface boundary: the device manager tracks status but does not forcibly kill containers when hardware fails.\n\n### 4.7 DRA vs. Device Plugin Coexistence\n\nBoth DRA (`draManager`) and classic device plugins (`deviceManager`) can be active simultaneously (controlled by the `DynamicResourceAllocation` feature gate). They use different plugin registration handlers (`DRAPlugin` vs. `DevicePlugin` in `GetPluginRegistrationHandlers`). A resource requested via DRA claims is handled entirely by `draManager.GetResources()` and never passes through `deviceManager`. The two paths do not interfere but cannot share a device instance.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Extension Points, Customization Hooks, and Associated Risks\n\n### 5.1 CPU Manager Policies via Options\n\nThe static CPU policy accepts a `CPUManagerPolicyOptions` map passed at kubelet startup. Options such as `full-pcpus-only`, `distribute-cpus-across-numa`, and `prefer-align-cpus-by-uncore-cache` alter the `cpuAccumulator` allocation strategy in `cpu_assignment.go` without requiring a different policy name. Adding a new option only requires implementing a new code path inside `policy_static.go:Allocate` and declaring the option in the feature-gated options registry.\n\n**Risk**: Options interact. `distribute-cpus-across-numa` and `full-pcpus-only` together may produce no valid allocation on nodes with odd CPU counts, causing guaranteed pods to be perpetually rejected. There is no static validation of option combinations; failures surface only at runtime as admission errors.\n\n### 5.2 Topology Manager Policies\n\nFour policy implementations exist in `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/`: `none`, `best-effort`, `restricted`, `single-numa-node`. They share the `HintMerger` in `policy.go` but differ in how they handle non-preferred hints. New policies can be added by implementing the `Policy` interface (`Name() string; Merge(logger, providersHints) (TopologyHint, bool)`) and registering in `NewManager`.\n\n**Risk**: A custom policy that always returns `preferred=true` for any affinity mask will never reject a pod, silently undermining NUMA locality guarantees without surfacing errors.\n\n### 5.3 Topology Manager Scopes\n\nTwo scopes exist (`scope_container.go`, `scope_pod.go`). Pod scope aggregates hints across all containers before merging; container scope merges per-container independently. Switching from container to pod scope on a running node with allocated Guaranteed pods requires draining the node, because pod scope may accept pods that container scope would reject (or vice versa).\n\n### 5.4 Device Plugins\n\nThird-party device plugins implement the v1beta1 gRPC API in `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/plugin/v1beta1/`. Plugins must implement `ListAndWatch`, `Allocate`, and optionally `GetTopologyHints`. The device manager calls `GetTopologyHints` during the topology manager's admission phase; a plugin that returns inaccurate NUMA topology hints will cause the topology manager to make suboptimal placements without any error signal.\n\nPlugins that return non-reproducible allocations across `Allocate` calls break the checkpoint restore invariant: after kubelet restart, the checkpoint may contain a device ID that the plugin no longer knows about.\n\n### 5.5 `HintProvider` Registration\n\n`cm.topologyManager.AddHintProvider(logger, provider)` in `container_manager_linux.go:312,341,356` is called during construction. This is not a runtime extension point; there is no way to register a new hint provider after `NewContainerManager` returns. In-tree managers are the only registered providers today.\n\n### 5.6 `InternalContainerLifecycle` Hooks\n\n`PreCreateContainer` in `internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go` is the place where CRI container config is modified (e.g., to inject CPU affinity annotations). Adding a new resource type that needs CRI-level config injection requires extending this hook. The interface is internal (`package cm`) and not a stable extension point for external components.\n\n---\n\n## 6. Operational Implications\n\n### 6.1 Performance\n\n**Admission latency**: Every pod admission call traverses the topology manager, which in turn calls `GetTopologyHints` on all registered providers synchronously. For a cluster with 3 resource managers and up to 8 NUMA nodes, the worst-case hint permutation count is O((2^8)^3) \u2248 16M. In practice, constraints reduce this by orders of magnitude, but nodes with many NUMA nodes and many resource types will exhibit measurable admission latency. Monitor `kubelet_topology_manager_admission_duration_ms` (emitted via `metrics`) to detect regressions.\n\n**CPU reconcile loop overhead**: The reconcile loop calls `runtimeService.UpdateContainerResources()` for every container whose CPU assignment differs from the last recorded state. On a node with hundreds of containers, the loop may generate significant CRI traffic every `CPUManagerReconcilePeriod`. Widen the period (`--cpu-manager-reconcile-period`) to reduce CRI overhead if CPU affinity changes are infrequent in your workload.\n\n**QoS cgroup update interval**: `qosContainerManager` recomputes burstable and best-effort cgroup limits every minute. On a busy node with rapidly changing pod counts, this update may arrive 60 s after a pod terminates, leaving residual memory headroom unclaimed. This is acceptable for typical workloads but can cause transient OOM pressure immediately after high-density pod churn.\n\n### 6.2 Reliability\n\n**Kubelet restart recovery**: CPU and memory managers restore state from checkpoint before `sourcesReady` is declared. Until sources are ready, stale state removal is suppressed (`reconcileState` skips removal). This window ensures that containers started by a prior kubelet invocation are not deprived of their CPU affinity during the brief period when pod sources are still being synchronized.\n\n**Device plugin disconnection**: The device manager uses gRPC streaming (`ListAndWatch`) to receive device health updates. If the plugin crashes or disconnects, its devices are immediately marked unhealthy in the manager's internal map. Capacity reported via `GetDevicePluginResourceCapacity` drops to zero for that resource, preventing new pods from being scheduled to the node. Running pods retain their device files \u2014 the kernel does not revoke them \u2014 but the plugin is no longer available to handle `Allocate` for new containers.\n\n**cgroup write failures**: `enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups` retries memory limit writes indefinitely when running pods' memory exceeds the target limit. This is the intended behavior: forcibly setting a lower limit would immediately OOM-kill running workloads. However, if eviction is misconfigured or stalled, the node will emit repeated `FailedNodeAllocatableEnforcement` events without ever converging. Operators should monitor for this event as a signal that eviction thresholds need tuning.\n\n### 6.3 Maintainability\n\n**Platform divergence**: `containerManagerImpl` has separate concrete implementations under build tags `linux`, `windows`, and the stub (`container_manager_stub.go`). The Windows implementation (`container_manager_windows.go`) contains a minimal struct with `deviceManager`, `topologyManager`, `cpuManager`, and `memoryManager` fields, but cgroup enforcement and QoS hierarchy are absent. Any change to `ContainerManager` interface in `container_manager.go` must be reflected in all three platform implementations. The mock in `pkg/kubelet/cm/testing/mocks.go` is auto-generated from the interface and must be regenerated after interface changes (`//go:generate mockery` in `container_manager.go:17`).\n\n**State schema evolution**: Checkpoint files embed a policy name. If the policy name changes (e.g., a rename of `\"static\"` to `\"pinned\"`), kubelet will consider the checkpoint invalid and reset all allocations at startup. This means all Guaranteed pods must be restarted to regain exclusive CPU/memory pinning after such a policy rename.\n\n---\n\n## 7. File and Directory Map\n\n| Path | Role |\n|---|---|\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go` | `ContainerManager` interface definition; `NodeConfig`, `NodeAllocatableConfig`, `Status` types |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go` | Linux `containerManagerImpl`: construction, `Start()`, wiring of sub-managers |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_windows.go` | Windows stub: minimal `containerManagerImpl`, no cgroup hierarchy |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_stub.go` | Unsupported-platform stub |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go` | `CgroupManager`, `PodContainerManager`, `CgroupName`, `ResourceConfig`, `CgroupConfig` interfaces and types |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle.go` | `InternalContainerLifecycle` interface; `internalContainerLifecycleImpl` implementation |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go` | Linux-specific `PreCreateContainer` (CRI config injection) |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_manager_linux.go` | `CgroupName.ToSystemd()`, name escaping, `NewCgroupManager` dispatch |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go` | cgroupfs v1 CRUD operations |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go` | cgroupfs v2 unified hierarchy CRUD operations |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go` | `QOSContainerManager`: Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort cgroup management |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/node_container_manager_linux.go` | Node allocatable cgroup creation and enforcement with retry logic |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/pod_container_manager_linux.go` | `PodContainerManager`: per-pod cgroup lifecycle |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/cpu_manager.go` | CPU `Manager` interface; `manager` struct; reconcile loop |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/policy_static.go` | Static CPU policy: `Allocate`, `allocateCPUs`, NUMA-aware assignment |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/cpu_assignment.go` | `cpuAccumulator`: topology-aware CPU selection algorithm |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/state/state_checkpoint.go` | Checkpoint-backed state persistence |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/memory_manager.go` | Memory `Manager` interface and implementation |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/policy_static.go` | Static memory policy: NUMA-pinned allocation |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/topology_manager.go` | `Manager` and `HintProvider` interfaces; `TopologyAffinityError`; NUMA node limit |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/policy.go` | `HintMerger`; hint permutation and scoring logic |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/scope_container.go` | Container-scope admission logic |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/scope_pod.go` | Pod-scope admission logic |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/bitmask/bitmask.go` | NUMA affinity bitmask operations |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/manager.go` | Device plugin `Manager` implementation; health tracking; allocation |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/pod_devices.go` | Per-pod device allocation tracking and checkpoint |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/topology_hints.go` | Device topology hint generation |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/plugin/v1beta1/` | Device plugin gRPC server/client and registration |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/` | Dynamic Resource Allocation manager (feature-gated) |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/resourceupdates/` | `Update` type for device/DRA health change events |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/admission/errors.go` | Admission error helpers |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/testing/mocks.go` | Auto-generated mock for `ContainerManager` (used in unit tests) |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/containermap/` | `ContainerMap` type: maps container IDs to pod UID + name during restart recovery |\n| `pkg/kubelet/cm/util/` | Helpers: cgroup path utilities |\n",
  "filePath": "/workspace/documentation.md",
  "originalFile": null,
  "structuredPatch": [],
  "type": "create"
}

      

      

        Code Changes (1)
        
1. WRITE /workspace/documentation.md
# Kubelet Container Manager: Subsystem Architecture Guide

## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

### Purpose

The kubelet container manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm`) is the subsystem responsible for translating pod resource specifications into kernel-enforced constraints on the node. Its core responsibilities are:

- Structuring the cgroup hierarchy that every pod and container lives under
- Enforcing node-level resource reservations (system, kube-reserved, node allocatable)
- Coordinating CPU, memory, and device allocation across competing pods
- Providing topology-aware placement on multi-NUMA nodes
- Bridging third-party hardware (via device plugins and DRA) into the container runtime

### Boundaries

**Upstream consumers:**
- `pkg/kubelet/kubelet.go` constructs the manager via `cm.NewContainerManager` and calls `ContainerManager.Start()`.
- `pkg/kubelet/lifecycle` calls `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` to gate pod admission.
- Pod workers call `InternalContainerLifecycle().PreCreateContainer()` / `PreStartContainer()` / `PostStopContainer()` at each container lifecycle event.

**Downstream dependencies:**
- `github.com/opencontainers/cgroups` — kernel cgroup interactions (v1 and v2)
- `k8s.io/cri-api/pkg/apis` (`RuntimeService`) — container runtime calls for resource updates
- `github.com/google/cadvisor` — machine topology and capacity discovery
- `pkg/kubelet/pluginmanager` — device plugin and DRA plugin registration

### What the Manager Does Not Do

- It does not schedule or select which node a pod lands on (that is the scheduler's role).
- It does not execute containers — that is the container runtime's role.
- It does not handle network or storage resource classes; those are separate kubelet subsystems.

---
